generated_by: AI-assisted public-source research; person-platform GTM repair 2026-06-17
disclaimer: This is not a normal brand SKU report. It maps product methodology to commercialization paths. Unsupported founder cash, exact margins, live valuations, private-company losses, or platform fee rates are kept in research_gaps.

founder_background: Steve Jobs: Apple co-founder, NeXT founder and Pixar majority owner; best studied as a product-methodology operator.
Steve Wozniak: Technical creator of Apple I/II; needed to keep Jobs product-commercial role separate from invention role.
Ronald Wayne: Early co-founder who exited quickly; useful for founding-history boundary.
brand_timeline: 1976: first_order 50 units x $500 - Byte Shop order created the first real Apple I commercial pull.
1977: angel_credit $92K + $250K credit line - Mike Markkula invested and helped secure credit, according to public Apple history.
1985: NeXT_funding $7M - Jobs founded NeXT with about $7M after leaving Apple.
1986: Pixar_purchase $5M purchase + $5M investment - Jobs bought Lucasfilm’s computer graphics division and invested additional capital, later Pixar.
1996-1997: NeXT_acquisition $427M - Apple acquired NeXT, bringing Jobs and NeXTSTEP back into Apple.
2006: Pixar_sale $7.4B - Disney acquired Pixar, making Jobs Disney’s largest individual shareholder.

founder_profiles.name: Steve Jobs
founder_profiles.background_summary: Apple co-founder, NeXT founder and Pixar majority owner; best studied as a product-methodology operator.

capital_history.description: Byte Shop order created the first real Apple I commercial pull.
Mike Markkula invested and helped secure credit, according to public Apple history.
Jobs founded NeXT with about $7M after leaving Apple.
Jobs bought Lucasfilm’s computer graphics division and invested additional capital, later Pixar.
Apple acquired NeXT, bringing Jobs and NeXTSTEP back into Apple.
Disney acquired Pixar, making Jobs Disney’s largest individual shareholder.

research_gaps: Do not write exact product margins without Apple filings.
Do not over-attribute Wozniak’s engineering to Jobs.
Jobs personal net worth should be sourced by date if used.

gtm_extension.summary: Steve Jobs should be mapped as a product-methodology case: end-to-end control, taste as strategy, category reframing, launch theater and ecosystem monetization. His commercialization path moved from Apple I hobbyist demand to Apple II scale, Macintosh design premium, NeXT technology salvage, Pixar storytelling economics, and finally iPod/iPhone/App Store platform economics. The report should not reduce Jobs to a single Apple SKU or generic founder myth.
gtm_extension.one_sentence_gtm: Steve Jobs should be mapped as a product-methodology case: end-to-end control, taste as strategy, category reframing, launch theater and ecosystem monetization.
channel_path: Apple II proves mass personal-computer business
Macintosh / NeXT build integrated design philosophy
iPod / iPhone / App Store turn product taste into ecosystem economics

channels.product_method: The person creates a repeatable product principle, not just a single product launch.
channels.platform: The strongest outputs become platforms, ecosystems, or distribution standards.
channels.commercialization: Monetization appears through hardware margin, services, commissions, ads, subscriptions, contracts, or enterprise fees.
channels.community: Developers, merchants, fans, users, channels, or government customers become the adoption network.
channels.governance: Platform risk comes from rules, support, safety, regulation, succession, and founder overreach.
channels.launch_theater: Keynotes and product reveals were distribution assets.
channels.retail_ecosystem: Apple Store, iTunes and App Store converted product love into controlled commerce.
channels.developer_platform: App Store turned iPhone from device into platform.

business_model.pricing_logic: Use premium pricing when the product reframes the category and reduces user complexity.
business_model.channel_economics: Control the full stack so margins, experience and developer distribution stay inside the ecosystem.

gtm_extension.top_products_selection_rule: For person/platform reports, choose no more than 3 product-methodology platforms, not ordinary SKUs.

top_products.name: Apple I -> Apple II: hobbyist demand to packaged computer
top_products.why_selected: This shows Jobs turning Wozniak’s engineering into a sellable product.
top_products.origin_story: Wozniak built the technical core; Jobs pushed packaging, sales and company formation.
top_products.pre_market: The Byte Shop order forced Apple to deliver assembled computers rather than loose hobby boards.
top_products.innovation: Apple II reframed personal computing as an appliance-like consumer/business product.
top_products.breakout: It created Apple’s first scale business.
top_products.customer_impact: Customers paid for a usable product, not only technical possibility.
top_products.ecosystem_impact: This is the origin of Jobs’s pattern: simplify and package complexity.

pricing_model.product_price: Apple I listed at $666.66; Byte Shop first order was 50 units at $500 each wholesale; Apple II launched at premium microcomputer pricing.
pricing_model.subscription_fee: No subscription/software fee; monetization was hardware sale and later software ecosystem around the platform.
pricing_model.accessory_service_fee: Accessory/service price: monitor, cassette/disk drive, expansion cards, software and support were part of total ownership cost.
pricing_model.competitor_price: Competitor price comparison: Altair/IMSAI kits were cheaper hobbyist options; Apple’s premium came from assembly, usability and packaging.

what_to_learn: Study the product principle first, then the commercialization path.
Separate the person from the company balance sheet and from platform economics.
For platforms, price often appears as commission, ad auction, subscription, service fee, or switching cost rather than SKU price.
do_not_copy_blindly: Do not force a normal brand template onto a person or platform report.
Do not invent founder cash, margin, loss, valuation, or fee rates when sources are weak.

top_products.name: iPod / iPhone / App Store platform
top_products.why_selected: This is Jobs’s strongest product-to-platform commercialization path.
top_products.origin_story: After returning to Apple, Jobs simplified the product line, then expanded from Mac to music to phone.
top_products.pre_market: Users wanted fewer devices and easier media/mobile internet experiences.
top_products.innovation: iPod + iTunes solved music; iPhone solved phone + internet; App Store solved developer distribution.
top_products.breakout: The iPhone/App Store became a recurring platform business after Jobs’s era.
top_products.customer_impact: Users paid premium hardware prices and then kept buying apps, services and accessories.
top_products.ecosystem_impact: It became the global template for mobile ecosystem monetization.

pricing_model.product_price: Original iPhone launched at $499/$599 with carrier contract; current iPhone prices vary by model and storage on Apple official store.
pricing_model.subscription_fee: App Store monetization historically used 30% commission, with 15% Small Business Program for eligible developers.
pricing_model.accessory_service_fee: Accessory/service price: cases, chargers, AppleCare, carrier plans, storage upgrades and app purchases shape total cost.
pricing_model.competitor_price: Competitor price comparison: BlackBerry/Nokia/Palm sold devices; Google Android gave OS to OEMs; Apple captured integrated hardware plus platform economics.

top_products.name: NeXT + Pixar: failure salvage and creative platform economics
top_products.why_selected: Jobs’s non-Apple years show how failure and taste became future leverage.
top_products.origin_story: After leaving Apple, Jobs funded NeXT and bought Pixar, neither a straightforward early win.
top_products.pre_market: NeXT hardware was expensive and niche; Pixar needed a business model beyond demos and hardware.
top_products.innovation: NeXTSTEP became the foundation for modern Apple OS; Pixar turned animation technology into story-driven studio economics.
top_products.breakout: Apple bought NeXT for $427M; Disney bought Pixar for $7.4B.
top_products.customer_impact: Customers did not buy NeXT at scale, but Apple later bought the technology; audiences bought Pixar stories.
top_products.ecosystem_impact: The lesson is that technical failure can become platform infrastructure if the underlying system is valuable.

pricing_model.product_price: NeXT Computer launched at a very high workstation price; Apple acquired NeXT for $427M; Jobs bought Pixar from Lucasfilm for about $5M plus investment and Disney later acquired Pixar for $7.4B.
pricing_model.subscription_fee: No consumer subscription; monetization came from workstation sales, OS value, film box office, distribution deals and equity sale.
pricing_model.accessory_service_fee: Accessory/service price: NeXT required expensive workstation ecosystem; Pixar required production/distribution capital rather than consumer accessories.
pricing_model.competitor_price: Competitor price comparison: Sun/SGI workstations competed with NeXT; Disney/DreamWorks competed in animation; Jobs won through OS leverage and Pixar storytelling economics rather than SKU volume.

what_to_learn: Study the product principle first, then the commercialization path.
Separate the person from the company balance sheet and from platform economics.
For platforms, price often appears as commission, ad auction, subscription, service fee, or switching cost rather than SKU price.
do_not_copy_blindly: Do not force a normal brand template onto a person or platform report.
Do not invent founder cash, margin, loss, valuation, or fee rates when sources are weak.

learning_summary: Jobs’s GTM is taste plus full-stack control turned into platform economics.
The commercial path matters more than the myth: order, packaging, launch, retail, developers, services.
NeXT and Pixar show that apparent failure can become infrastructure or equity leverage.
gtm_extension.evidence_boundary: Missing founder cash, exact platform take rates, CAC, refund loss, warranty reserve, private valuation, or live net worth is not filled unless a reliable source is present.

sources.title: Apple App Store Small Business Program
